name: Honeywell Control Blocks for Siemens
desc: Honeywell DCS control blocks — PID, DEVCTL, AI/AO/DI/DO — ported to Siemens SCL for plants running Honeywell as the main DCS and Siemens PLCs as auxiliary units. Operators work with the same control semantics across both systems.
problem:
Plants running Honeywell DCS as the main system often use Siemens PLCs for auxiliary units — compressors, utilities, ancillary processes. Operators trained on Honeywell control semantics face a context switch every time they work on a Siemens unit: different block names, different parameter conventions, different behaviour under the same operating conditions.

This library ports the core Honeywell control modules to Siemens S7-300/1500 as native FC/FB blocks written in SCL and called from Ladder. PID, DEVCTL, and AI/AO/DI/DO behave the way Honeywell operators expect — same semantics, same tuning conventions, same alarm behaviour. No retraining. No dual documentation. One control philosophy across the plant.
audience: Automation engineers and system integrators at plants running Honeywell DCS alongside Siemens PLCs — where operational consistency across both systems matters more than platform purity.

name: Imbra Connect
desc: A unified industrial protocol SDK — one consistent interface for MQTT, Modbus, CAN, CANopen, DeviceNet, EtherNet/IP, and more. Python and Go are open source (MIT). Ports to other languages are available on request. Built to power ImBrain agents and any connectivity project that should not start from scratch.
problem:
The Python industrial protocol ecosystem is fragmented, undermaintained, and incomplete. DeviceNet has no open library at all. EtherNet/IP lost its only maintained project. HART and Profibus have stale, barely active codebases. Engineers assembling a connectivity stack end up combining five different libraries with inconsistent APIs — and still hit gaps.

The Go ecosystem has the same problem from a different angle: not fragmentation, but absence. DeviceNet, HART, Profibus, and CANopen simply do not exist as Go libraries. Teams that chose Go for its performance and concurrency model hit a dead end the moment they need to talk to field devices.

Imbra Connect fills those gaps with a single package. One abstract interface — Connection, Driver, Adapter — works across all supported protocols. Adding a new protocol or swapping hardware does not change application code. Packet crafting and test tooling are first-class. Engineers can craft standard and non-standard frames, handle malformed packets, and build integration tests without specialised hardware.

Python and Go are fully open source (MIT), covering protocol implementations, packet crafting, agent scaffolding, and test tooling with no paid tiers or feature gates. Community agents are submitted to the Imbra registry. Open source under MIT is mandatory for submission.

Imbra Connect is the foundation layer for ImBrain agents. Every device ImBrain can talk to is a protocol implementation in Imbra Connect. Ports to other languages are available on request for teams that need embedded or browser targets.
audience: Automation engineers, embedded developers, and system integrators building device connectivity layers — and anyone writing agents for ImBrain or other industrial data platforms.

name: ImBrain™
desc: An open-architecture industrial historian with a plugin ecosystem and natural language interface. It stores time-series data, alarms, lab results, and virtual tags, connects plants in a mesh, and lets operators query everything in plain language. The core and agents are open source. Analytics plugins are paid.
problem:
Industrial historians fall into two categories: enterprise platforms priced at six figures with multi-year implementation projects, and siloed SCADA databases that cannot be queried, aggregated, or reasoned over. Small and medium plants are left with spreadsheets, manual shift reports, and no cross-site visibility.

ImBrain is built on an open hourglass architecture. Go-based agents form the collection layer, connecting any data source — PLCs, sensors, lab systems, WinCC alarms, legacy OPC DA servers — using the Imbra Connect SDK. Paid plugins sit above the core and deliver analytics (OEE, Golden Batch, Anomaly Detection, Energy, SPC), cloud forwarding with hot-path streaming and cold Parquet archives, and a natural language interface powered by a local or cloud LLM.

Multiple ImBrain instances connect in a mesh over MQTT/Sparkplug B — enabling cross-site queries, hierarchical aggregation, and enterprise-wide dashboards without moving raw data to a central server. The user can just ask "Show me the KPI scores of all soda ash plants in France" and ImBrain™ will do the rest.

The core stores four record types — numeric tags with configurable downsampling (1s → 10s → 1min → 1h → 1day), text tags, alarm events with full lifecycle, and LIMS lab results. Virtual tags — including totalizers, rolling statistics, and expressions — are computed on each incoming sample with no polling lag.

ImBrain ships as a self-contained installer that bundles everything needed to run — no cloud dependency, no external licence server. It runs air-gapped on a $500 industrial mini PC and scales without architectural changes to enterprise mesh deployments spanning hundreds of sites. Enterprise deployments are fully automation-friendly — the client triggers the update via Ansible or Salt, ImBrain owns everything that happens after.
audience: Operations engineers and plant managers at small and medium industrial companies who need historian capabilities without enterprise licensing costs. Any organisation with physical sensors, continuous processes, and compliance requirements — manufacturing, energy, utilities, food and beverage, mining, building automation — fits the same pattern. Also a fit for enterprise teams looking to replace closed, vendor-locked historian platforms with an open, extensible architecture.
